wines.name: Benanti Pietramarina
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel only; no oak

taste.summary: The benchmark Etna Bianco Superiore from Milo: Carricante of crystalline mineral purity, volcanic salinity, and piercing acidity that rewards 8-plus years of cellaring.

pairings.why: Volcanic salinity echoes the capers; racy acidity cuts the oily fish.
The wine's briny mineral core pairs perfectly with oceanic richness.
Bright acidity lifts the soft cheese; citrus aromatics harmonise.

history.summary: Pietramarina comes from the Milo contrada on Etna's east slope, one of the few zones entitled to the Etna Bianco Superiore denomination. Benanti revived Carricante vinification in the 1980s-1990s after decades of decline; Pietramarina debuted with the 1990 vintage and remains Sicily's most celebrated white wine.

milestones.event: First Pietramarina vintage released, establishing Milo Carricante as a serious ageing white
Etna DOC regulations amended to recognise Superiore sub-denomination for Milo whites

wines.description: Benchmark Etna Bianco Superiore from Milo on Etna's east slope: pure Carricante of volcanic mineral purity and racy acidity, first released in 1990.

wines.name: Benanti Rovittello
wines.oak_regime: 24 months in large Slavonian oak botti

taste.summary: From Rovittello on the north slope, this Benanti red shows the classic Etna paradox: light ruby colour but profound mineral depth, firm tannins and soaring acidity demanding patient cellaring.

pairings.dish: Braised rabbit with rosemary and olives
pairings.why: Firm tannins and herb savouriness complement the gamey richness.
The wine's iron-and-cherry profile cuts through pork fat cleanly.
Sweet-and-sour aubergine echoes the wine's tart cherry acidity.

history.summary: Rovittello is the north-slope contrada where Benanti farms pre-phylloxera Nerello Mascalese on alberello-trained vines. The cuvee was one of the first single-contrada Etna Rosso bottlings, predating the wave of single-vineyard bottlings that defined the Etna renaissance in the 2000s.

milestones.event: First Rovittello vintage, among Sicily's earliest single-contrada Etna Rosso releases

wines.description: Single-contrada Etna Rosso from Rovittello on Etna's north slope: Nerello Mascalese of Burgundian delicacy, volcanic mineral depth and firm tannic structure.

wines.name: Benanti Serra della Contessa
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in 500-litre tonneaux

taste.summary: Benanti's top-tier Etna Rosso from the Serra della Contessa contrada at 700 metres: concentrated, structured, and among the most age-worthy expressions of Nerello Mascalese on the volcano.

pairings.dish: Bistecca alla siciliana with charcoal herbs
pairings.why: Graphite and charcoal notes in the wine mirror the char; firm tannins cut the meat.
Volcanic minerality and earthy mushroom create a compelling umami resonance.
The wine's structure balances the hard cheese's intensity.

history.summary: Serra della Contessa is sourced from Benanti's oldest Nerello Mascalese vines in the contrada of the same name on the south-east slope of Etna. The wine was created to represent the most complex and age-worthy expression of the estate, and is released only in the best vintages.

milestones.event: First Serra della Contessa vintage; declassified to IGT in weaker years

wines.description: Benanti's prestige Etna Rosso from old Nerello Mascalese vines in the Serra della Contessa contrada: concentrated, structured, and built for long cellaring.

wines.name: Benanti Etna Rosato
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel only

taste.summary: A pale, delicate Etna rosato from Nerello Mascalese: vibrant red-fruit aromatics, high acidity and a saline volcanic finish typical of the east-slope terroir.

pairings.why: The rosato's delicate fruit and salinity echo the sweet red mullet flesh.
Fresh acidity cuts the fried richness; the wine's light body refreshes.
Bright red-berry notes harmonise with the tomato's sweet acidity.

history.summary: Benanti's Etna Rosato was introduced as consumer interest in Sicilian rose wines grew in the mid-2000s, showcasing the pale, elegant style that Nerello Mascalese produces when briefly skin-contacted. The wine captures the volcanic energy of the east slope in an immediately approachable format.

milestones.event: First rosato vintage added to the Benanti range

wines.description: Pale Etna Rosato from Nerello Mascalese on Benanti's east-slope vineyards: vibrant red-fruit aromatics, volcanic salinity and crisp acidity.

wines.name: Tenuta delle Terre Nere Calderara Sottana
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in large Slavonian oak botti (30 hl)

taste.summary: Calderara Sottana is a benchmark for north-slope Etna Rosso: Burgundian elegance with volcanic minerality, tart red-fruit purity and the structural firmness that demands 8-plus years.

pairings.why: The wine's firm tannins and iron notes frame the lamb's gamey richness.
Volcanic earthiness and the wine's mineral core mirror the truffle's umami.
Salty, crystalline sheep's-milk cheese sharpens the wine's red-cherry lift.

history.summary: Marc de Grazia established Tenuta delle Terre Nere on Etna's north slope in 2002 and launched single-contrada bottlings from the 2003 vintage, making this one of the earliest single-contrada Etna Rosso cuvees in the modern era. Calderara Sottana, from vines planted between 1920 and 1945, became the estate's signature expression.

milestones.event: First Calderara Sottana vintage; established contrada bottling as the future of Etna DOC

wines.description: One of Etna's defining single-contrada reds: old-vine Nerello Mascalese from Calderara Sottana on the north slope, Burgundian in structure and volcanic in soul.

verified.address_quoted: Contrada Calderara, sn 95036 Randazzo (CT) - Sicilia - Italia

wines.name: Tenuta delle Terre Nere Guardiola
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in large Slavonian oak botti (30 hl)

taste.summary: The Guardiola contrada yields the most powerful and structured of the Terre Nere single-vineyard range: darker fruit, greater density, and a long mineral-chalky finish.

pairings.why: The wine's density and graphite structure match the rich braise; Marsala echoes the volcanic character.
Gamey ragu resonates with the wine's dark-fruit intensity and earthy mineral core.
The firm tannins and acidity cut through the rich, crystalline cheese.

history.summary: Guardiola is the highest-altitude contrada in the Terre Nere portfolio, at approximately 900 metres on the north slope. The vineyard's pre-war Nerello vines on sandy volcanic soils produce the estate's most concentrated and long-lived wine, often regarded as the top cuvee in the range.

milestones.event: First Guardiola vintage alongside Calderara Sottana and San Lorenzo

wines.description: The most powerful Terre Nere single-contrada red: old-vine Nerello Mascalese from the high-altitude Guardiola contrada, dense and built for long ageing.

verified.address_quoted: Contrada Calderara, sn 95036 Randazzo (CT) - Sicilia - Italia

wines.name: Tenuta delle Terre Nere San Lorenzo
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in large Slavonian oak botti (30 hl)

taste.summary: San Lorenzo's lower-altitude vines yield a more accessible Terre Nere red: fragrant, iron-streaked, with vibrant sour-cherry acidity and a mineral iron finish.

pairings.dish: Pasta al sugo di pomodoro fresco
pairings.why: The wine's tomato-like acidity and cherry fruit harmonise beautifully with fresh tomato sauce.
The lighter body and acidity pair elegantly with delicate fish.
Red-fruit brightness and iron notes cut through cured pork fat.

history.summary: The San Lorenzo contrada is the most accessible of the Terre Nere single-contrada range, with vines at lower elevations producing a more immediately approachable style than Guardiola or Calderara. The 2003 vintage was the launch year for the entire single-contrada programme.

milestones.event: First San Lorenzo vintage as part of the inaugural Terre Nere contrada series

wines.description: Approachable single-contrada Etna Rosso from the San Lorenzo plot on Etna's north slope: fragrant, iron-tinged Nerello with vibrant sour-cherry acidity.

verified.address_quoted: Contrada Calderara, sn 95036 Randazzo (CT) - Sicilia - Italia

wines.name: Tenuta delle Terre Nere Feudo di Mezzo
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in large Slavonian oak botti (30 hl)

taste.summary: Feudo di Mezzo is the most aromatic and perfumed of the Terre Nere lineup: complex, multi-layered, with chalky depth and remarkable persistence.

pairings.why: The wine's dark-cherry intensity and cedar notes pair classically with duck.
Firm tannins and herb savouriness handle the gamey richness of venison.
Chalky mineral depth and dried-rose aromatics resonate with black truffle.

history.summary: Feudo di Mezzo is the highest-elevation Terre Nere contrada at around 950 metres on the north slope, added to the portfolio in 2007. The cooler temperatures and older vines produce the estate's most aromatic and complex expression, often considered a rival to Guardiola for top-billing.

milestones.event: Feudo di Mezzo added to the contrada range as the fourth single-vineyard cuvee

wines.description: High-altitude Etna Rosso from the Feudo di Mezzo contrada at 950m on Etna's north slope: aromatic, complex, chalky-mineral, from Terre Nere's 2007-added cru.

verified.address_quoted: Contrada Calderara, sn 95036 Randazzo (CT) - Sicilia - Italia

wines.name: Tenuta delle Terre Nere Etna Rosso
wines.oak_regime: 12 months in large Slavonian oak botti

taste.summary: The estate Etna Rosso represents the breadth of Terre Nere's north-slope holdings: an approachable, everyday expression of Nerello Mascalese with cherry freshness and volcanic minerality.

pairings.why: Classic Sicilian everyday pairing; the wine's cherry freshness matches tomato perfectly.
The wine's herb notes and light body complement without overpowering the fennel.
Light body and bright acidity cut through fried rice balls.

history.summary: The entry-level estate Etna Rosso was the first wine released by Tenuta delle Terre Nere on its founding in 2002, blending fruit from across the north-slope holdings. As the single-contrada range expanded, the estate red became the accessible introduction to the Terre Nere terroir philosophy.

milestones.event: First vintage produced as the inaugural Terre Nere release

wines.description: Terre Nere's accessible entry-level Etna Rosso: blended from north-slope holdings, bright cherry fruit, volcanic herbs and an honest everyday character.

verified.address_quoted: Contrada Calderara, sn 95036 Randazzo (CT) - Sicilia - Italia

wines.name: Passopisciaro Contrada C (Chiappemacine)
wines.oak_regime: 15 months in large French oak botti (25-50 hl)

taste.summary: Contrada C Chiappemacine is among the lightest and most elegant of the Passopisciaro contrada series: fragrant, iron-tinged, with firm but fine-grained tannins.

pairings.why: The wine's herb complexity and firm tannins pair beautifully with the slow-cooked pork.
Iron-mineral notes and volcanic earthiness echo the umami depth of wild mushroom.
The cheese's mild smokiness complements the wine's charcoal and dark-fruit notes.

history.summary: Andrea Franchetti introduced the single-contrada series for Passopisciaro starting with the 2007 vintage, labelling each wine with its contrada letter (C for Chiappemacine). Chiappemacine lies at around 700-800 metres on the north slope, planted to pre-war Nerello Mascalese on sandy volcanic soils.

milestones.event: Inaugural vintage of the Passopisciaro contrada series, redefining cru wine from Etna

wines.description: The most fragrant of the Passopisciaro contrada series: Contrada C Chiappemacine, old-vine Nerello Mascalese at 700-800m, iron-tinged and built for the cellar.

verified.address_quoted: Via Canonico Zumbo, 1 (hamlet of Passopisciaro) IT - 95012 Castiglione di Sicilia (CT) Etna Nord - Sicily

wines.name: Passopisciaro Contrada R (Rampante)
wines.oak_regime: 15 months in large French oak botti (25-50 hl)

taste.summary: Contrada R Rampante, from the highest-altitude Passopisciaro site at 1,000 metres, produces the most structured and slow-evolving wine of the range: taut, mineral and built for exceptional longevity.

pairings.why: The wine's chalk-mineral dryness and acidity elevate crystalline aged sheep cheese.

pairings.dish: Grilled Sicilian lamb with wild herbs
pairings.why: High-altitude intensity and firm structure frame the gamey lamb beautifully.
The wine's raw mineral power matches the bracing rawness of venison tartare.

history.summary: Rampante is the highest contrada in the Passopisciaro portfolio, named for the steep volcanic terrain at approximately 1,000 metres altitude. Andrea Franchetti considered it the most challenging to farm and the most rewarding to age, and it commands the highest prices in the contrada series.

milestones.event: First vintage in the inaugural contrada series launch

wines.description: The pinnacle of the Passopisciaro contrada range: Nerello Mascalese from the steep Rampante site at 1,000m, taut, chalk-mineral and extraordinarily long-lived.

verified.address_quoted: Via Canonico Zumbo, 1 (hamlet of Passopisciaro) IT - 95012 Castiglione di Sicilia (CT) Etna Nord - Sicily

wines.name: Passopisciaro Passorosso
wines.oak_regime: 12 months in large oak botti

taste.summary: The entry-level Passopisciaro: blended from multiple north-slope plots, fragrant and approachable, offering a compelling introduction to the estate's Nerello Mascalese philosophy.

pairings.why: The wine's bright acidity and light herbs bridge the sweet-savoury Sicilian classic.
Everyday pairing for the north-slope lamb tradition; the wine's lightness matches.
Light body and fresh acidity cut the fried rice ball without competing.

history.summary: Passorosso was the inaugural wine of Passopisciaro, produced from the first harvest on the Andrea Franchetti estate in 2001. It blends fruit from multiple contrade and is positioned as the accessible gateway to the estate's terroir philosophy before the single-contrada series launches.

milestones.event: First Passopisciaro vintage, introducing Nerello Mascalese at high altitude to an international audience

wines.description: Passopisciaro's entry-level Nerello Mascalese from multiple north-slope contrade: approachable, fragrant and a compelling introduction to high-altitude Etna Rosso.

verified.address_quoted: Via Canonico Zumbo, 1 (hamlet of Passopisciaro) IT - 95012 Castiglione di Sicilia (CT) Etna Nord - Sicily

wines.name: Passopisciaro Passobianco
wines.oak_regime: 8 months in large French oak botti (no new oak)

taste.summary: Chardonnay at 1,000 metres on Etna's volcanic soils: a rare and singular white - tropical richness undercut by volcanic salinity and a cool high-altitude acidity that gives exceptional freshness.

pairings.why: The wine's full body matches the meaty fish; mineral-savoury finish echoes the herb sauce.
Saline-mineral character bridges the briny clams; acidity brightens the dish.
The wine's richness and tropical notes pair elegantly with the creamy cheese.

history.summary: Andrea Franchetti introduced Chardonnay to Etna at approximately 1,000 metres with the Passobianco, inspired by the grape's performance in extreme continental climates. The volcanic soils and altitude produce a white radically different from both Burgundy and low-altitude Sicilian Chardonnay, becoming one of the island's most distinctive white wines.

milestones.event: First Passobianco vintage, establishing high-altitude Etna Chardonnay as a serious proposition

wines.description: Rare high-altitude Chardonnay from Passopisciaro at 1,000m on volcanic Etna soils: tropical richness undercut by volcanic salinity and cool acidity.

verified.address_quoted: Via Canonico Zumbo, 1 (hamlet of Passopisciaro) IT - 95012 Castiglione di Sicilia (CT) Etna Nord - Sicily

wines.name: Passopisciaro Franchetti
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in French oak barriques and botti

taste.summary: Franchetti is the prestige cuvee of the estate: a powerful blend of Petit Verdot and Cesanese d'Affile from Etna's volcanic soils, combining Bordeaux structure with the island's volcanic energy. A wine of exceptional concentration and longevity.

pairings.why: The wine's dense tannins and dark-fruit power call for the richest beef cuts.
The wine's body and depth match the richness of marrow-filled slow-cooked veal shank.
The wine's firm tannins and graphite depth pair with the crystalline intensity of aged cheese.

history.summary: Andrea Franchetti, who established Tenuta di Trinoro in Tuscany before turning to Etna, created the Franchetti cuvee as his flagship expression on the volcano. Blending Petit Verdot and Cesanese d'Affile - two grapes rarely seen in Sicily - at altitude above 1,000 metres, the wine became a cult bottling commanding some of the highest prices of any Etna wine.

milestones.event: First Franchetti vintage, merging Bordeaux varieties with Etna volcanic terroir

wines.description: Passopisciaro's prestige cuvee: Petit Verdot and Cesanese d'Affile at 1,000m on volcanic Etna soils, a cult wine of exceptional density and longevity.

verified.address_quoted: Via Canonico Zumbo, 1 (hamlet of Passopisciaro) IT - 95012 Castiglione di Sicilia (CT) Etna Nord - Sicily

wines.name: Graci Arcuria
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in large Slavonian oak botti (20-30 hl)

taste.summary: Arcuria is the Graci flagship: from 52 acres of ungrafted pre-phylloxera vines on the north slope, it epitomises Etna's Burgundian paradox - light ruby colour belying deep mineral complexity and firm, precise tannins.

pairings.dish: Grilled lamb cutlets with wild thyme
pairings.why: The wine's mineral-herb complexity mirrors wild thyme; firm tannins frame the lamb.
Aromatic herbs in the wine pair naturally with fennel-scented roast pork.
Volcanic earthiness and dried-herb notes resonate with porcini's umami depth.

history.summary: Alberto Aiello Graci purchased old-vine Nerello parcels at Passopisciaro in 2004, farming the ungrafted pre-phylloxera vines biodynamically. Arcuria, named for the contrada, quickly became one of the most sought-after single-vineyard expressions on Etna, with production limited to the best vintages.

milestones.event: First Arcuria vintage from ungrafted north-slope vines

wines.description: Graci's benchmark Etna Rosso from 52 acres of ungrafted pre-phylloxera Nerello Mascalese on the north slope: deep mineral complexity in a Burgundian silhouette.

verified.address_quoted: Contrada Feudo di Mezzo, SP7iii, Passopisciaro 95012, Castiglione di Sicilia(CT)

wines.name: Graci Barbabecchi
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in large Slavonian oak botti

taste.summary: From the Barbabecchi contrada at higher elevation than Arcuria, this Graci red is the most structured and demanding of the range: dense, chalky, and extraordinarily long-lived.

pairings.dish: Slow-braised oxtail with tomato and olives
pairings.why: The wine's density and firm tannins call for the richest, most gelatinous meat dishes.
Chalky mineral depth and incense notes resonate with black truffle's earthiness.
Dense tannins and high acidity balance the crystalline, sharp aged cheese.

history.summary: Barbabecchi is Graci's second single-contrada bottling, added to the range in 2010 from a higher-altitude plot than Arcuria. The site's cooler temperatures and older soils produce a more structured and austere expression, considered by many critics the estate's most age-worthy wine.

milestones.event: First Barbabecchi vintage, expanding the Graci single-contrada range

wines.description: Graci's most structured single-contrada Etna Rosso from the high-altitude Barbabecchi plot: dense, chalky-mineral, built for exceptional cellaring.

verified.address_quoted: Contrada Feudo di Mezzo, SP7iii, Passopisciaro 95012, Castiglione di Sicilia(CT)

wines.name: Graci Etna Bianco
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel only

taste.summary: A bright, citrus-driven Etna Bianco blending Carricante and Catarratto: saline, mineral, refreshing - capturing the north slope's volcanic energy in a fresh and approachable white.

pairings.why: Briny salinity echoes the sea-fresh octopus; bright acidity cuts the oil.
Classic pairing: mineral-saline character and bright acidity bridge the bivalve sweetness.
Refreshing acidity lifts the creamy ricotta; citrus notes harmonise.

history.summary: Graci introduced its Etna Bianco to complement the red range, drawing on Carricante and Catarratto from north-slope holdings. The wine is positioned as an accessible, everyday expression of Etna's white-wine terroir, before the single-contrada Carricante ambitions of the estate mature.

milestones.event: First Graci Etna Bianco vintage, establishing the white-wine range

wines.description: Bright, mineral-driven Etna Bianco from Graci: Carricante and Catarratto on the north slope, saline-citrus and refreshingly approachable.

wines.name: Graci Etna Rosso
wines.oak_regime: 12 months in large Slavonian oak botti

taste.summary: The entry Graci Etna Rosso offers immediate pleasure: Nerello Mascalese at its most approachable - fragrant, herb-scented, with the estate's characteristic saline mineral finish.

pairings.dish: Pasta al norma (eggplant, tomato, ricotta salata)
pairings.why: Classic Sicilian pairing: the wine's bright cherry and herb notes match perfectly.
Light body and refreshing acidity complement grilled oily fish without overwhelming.
The wine's fragrance bridges sweet melon and salty cured ham.

history.summary: The entry-level Graci Etna Rosso was produced from the estate's first harvest in 2004, drawing on blended fruit from across the north-slope holdings as a more accessible companion to the single-contrada wines.

milestones.event: First vintage alongside the inaugural Arcuria release

wines.description: Approachable Graci Etna Rosso from blended north-slope holdings: fragrant, herb-scented Nerello Mascalese with a saline mineral finish.

wines.name: Pietradolce Archineri Etna Bianco
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel; 6 months on fine lees

taste.summary: Archineri is Pietradolce's benchmark Etna Bianco Superiore: pure Carricante of nervy mineral tension, citrus brightness and the chalky volcanic finesse that defines Milo-area white wines.

pairings.dish: Grilled sea bream with lemon and caper
pairings.why: The mineral purity and bright citrus notes bridge the clean flavours of sea bream.

pairings.dish: Raw scampi with Sicilian olive oil
pairings.why: Saline character mirrors the oceanic sweetness; bright acidity highlights the olive oil.
Light herbaceous notes complement the fresh cheese; acidity keeps the combination clean.

history.summary: Michele Faro planted Carricante on the east slope in the Archineri contrada when establishing Pietradolce in 2005, aiming to produce a benchmark Etna Bianco Superiore comparable to Benanti's Pietramarina. The wine is named for the contrada and has established itself among the finest white wines produced on the volcano.

Tre Bicchieri from Gambero Rosso for the 2014 vintage

wines.description: Pietradolce's benchmark Etna Bianco Superiore from the Archineri contrada: pure Carricante of mineral tension, citrus brightness and volcanic chalky finesse.

wines.name: Pietradolce Vigna Barbagalli
wines.oak_regime: 24 months in large Slavonian oak botti (25 hl)

taste.summary: Vigna Barbagalli is Pietradolce's prestige single-vineyard Etna Rosso from ungrafted pre-phylloxera vines at 850 metres: among the most collectible and age-worthy Nerello Mascalese produced on the north slope.

pairings.dish: Grilled T-bone with Sicilian sea salt
pairings.why: The wine's dense graphite tannins and high acidity cut through the richest beef cuts.
Complex iron-mineral notes and firm structure perfectly frame gamey wild boar.
Volcanic earthiness and iron notes create a thrilling mineral-umami dialogue.

history.summary: Vigna Barbagalli was released from the 2012 vintage after Michele Faro identified exceptional ungrafted Nerello Mascalese vines in the Barbagalli contrada at 850 metres altitude. The very low yields from these old vines and the extended ageing regime produced a wine immediately regarded as among the finest on Etna.

wines.description: Pietradolce's prestige Etna Rosso from ungrafted pre-phylloxera Nerello Mascalese at 850m: one of the island's most collectible and age-worthy north-slope crus.

taste.summary: Pietradolce's entry Etna Rosso: approachable, herb-scented Nerello Mascalese with cherry brightness and Etna's characteristic saline mineral finish.

pairings.why: Bright cherry acidity mirrors sun-dried Sicilian tomato sweetness.
Light to medium body and herb notes pair naturally with grilled poultry.

pairings.dish: Bruschetta with fresh tomato and basil
pairings.why: The wine's freshness and light body match the casual simplicity of bruschetta.

history.summary: The entry-level Pietradolce Etna Rosso was introduced alongside the estate's founding in 2005, providing an approachable Nerello Mascalese expression from blended north-slope holdings before the single-vineyard range was established.

milestones.event: First Pietradolce Etna Rosso vintage alongside the estate launch

wines.description: Pietradolce's entry-level Etna Rosso: approachable, herb-scented Nerello Mascalese with cherry brightness and the estate's signature mineral finish.

wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel

taste.summary: A fresh, immediately drinkable Etna Bianco: citrus-forward with Carricante's characteristic volcanic salinity and clean mineral finish.

pairings.dish: Spaghetti con ricci di mare (sea urchin)
pairings.why: Briny salinity and citrus acidity complement the creamy oceanic richness of sea urchin.
Light body and bright acidity pair perfectly with simply prepared squid.
Refreshing acidity and mineral notes harmonise with mozzarella and ripe tomato.

history.summary: Pietradolce added an entry-level Etna Bianco to the range in 2010 to accompany the more complex Archineri Superiore, providing an everyday white wine expression from the Carricante-Catarratto blend characteristic of the north-slope white production.

wines.name: Girolamo Russo San Lorenzo
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in large Slavonian oak botti (20-30 hl)

taste.summary: San Lorenzo is the most immediately accessible of the Russo single-contrada range: fragrant, iron-streaked, with vibrant sour cherry and a saline volcanic freshness that makes it the most drinkable Russo Etna Rosso in its youth.

pairings.dish: Grilled tuna with capers and olives
pairings.why: The wine's bright acidity and iron notes pair beautifully with meaty tuna.
Classic north-Sicilian pairing; the wine's fruit and light body match perfectly.
Iron and cherry brightness complement cured Sicilian meats.

history.summary: Giuseppe Russo took over his father Girolamo's old Nerello vines in 2005 and launched single-contrada bottlings beginning with the 2007 vintage. San Lorenzo, named for the contrada at approximately 650 metres, is the most approachable of the range, bottled without fining or filtration.

milestones.event: First San Lorenzo vintage alongside the inaugural Russo contrada series

wines.description: Girolamo Russo's most approachable single-contrada Etna Rosso from the San Lorenzo plot: fragrant, iron-tinged and vibrant with sour cherry acidity.

wines.name: Girolamo Russo Feudo
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in large Slavonian oak botti (20-30 hl)

taste.summary: Feudo is the most structured of the Russo single-contrada range: darker-fruited, firmer in tannin, with a chalky mineral depth that demands patience but rewards handsomely.

pairings.why: The wine's density and firm tannins call for long-cooked, rich beef.
Chalky mineral depth and dark-fruit notes resonate with the earthy richness of porcini.
The wine's structure handles the intensity of this hard, crystalline Sicilian cheese.

history.summary: Feudo is the middle tier of the Russo single-contrada range, from a contrada at higher elevation than San Lorenzo. The wine is produced without fining or filtration, farmed organically, and represents the best balance between accessibility and the structured power of the estate's top parcels.

milestones.event: First Feudo vintage in the inaugural Russo contrada series

wines.description: Girolamo Russo's structured mid-range single-contrada Etna Rosso: darker fruit, firmer tannins, chalky mineral depth from organically farmed north-slope Nerello.

wines.name: Girolamo Russo 'A Rina
wines.oak_regime: 18 months in large Slavonian oak botti

taste.summary: The prestige Russo single-vineyard: 'A Rina (dialect for 'la rena' - the sand) from some of the deepest sandy volcanic soils on the north slope, producing the most austere and age-demanding wine in the portfolio.

The fourth-generation estate farms 12.5 hectares using only copper and sulphur, and the wine is produced without fining or filtration, embodying the philosophy of minimal intervention that the estate has practised for over a century.

wines.name: Murgo Brut Metodo Classico

The Murgo Brut remains Sicily's most acclaimed traditional-method sparkling wine and a landmark demonstration of the island's potential beyond still wines.

taste.summary: Magma is one of Italy's most sought-after cult wines: pure Nerello Mascalese from ungrafted pre-phylloxera vines above 1,000 metres, no sulphur added, aged in amphora. The wine embodies volcanic terroir at its most extreme and unfiltered.

history.summary: Belgian wine merchant Frank Cornelissen came to Etna in 2001 driven by a vision of extreme natural wine from extreme terroir. Magma, from ungrafted Nerello Mascalese vines above 1,000 metres in the Solicchiata zone, is fermented without sulphur in amphora and has become one of the most coveted and discussed natural wines in the world. Production is extremely limited.

Austere mineral-iron character matches the gamey complexity of wild boar ragu.

wines.name: Cantine Milazzo Mamertino Rosso
wines.oak_regime: 12 months in large oak botti

taste.summary: One of the few expressions of the rare Mamertino DOC: a Nero d'Avola and Nocera blend from the Milazzo hills near the Tyrrhenian, with the dense dark-fruit character of Nero d'Avola lifted by Nocera's bitter herb notes.

pairings.dish: Pasta alla norma (aubergine, tomato, salted ricotta)
pairings.why: Dark fruit and herbal bitterness pair well with this Sicilian pasta classic.

pairings.why: The wine's dense fruit and medium body complement grilled pork and sweet peppers.
Dark cherry density and herb notes bridge the fried rice ball's pork-ragu filling.

history.summary: Mamertino DOC, near Milazzo in the Messina province, is one of Sicily's most ancient wine appellations - Roman sources including Pliny the Elder recorded wines from this area. Cantine Milazzo is one of the few modern producers reviving this near-forgotten DOC, blending native Nero d'Avola and the even rarer Nocera grape.

milestones.event: Mamertino DOC officially recognised, reviving the ancient appellation name
First Cantine Milazzo Mamertino DOC bottling

wines.description: A rare Mamertino DOC expression from near Milazzo: Nero d'Avola and Nocera from an ancient Roman-era appellation, dark-fruited with herbal-bitter character.

wines.name: Cantine Milazzo Nero d'Avola
wines.oak_regime: 10 months in large oak botti

taste.summary: Cantine Milazzo's Sicilia DOC Nero d'Avola from Tyrrhenian-influenced north-east Sicily: accessible, dark-fruited and an everyday ambassador for this native grape at a coastal terroir.

pairings.why: The wine's dark-fruit density and medium body pair well with tomato-and-sausage pasta.
In the Messina area, tuna is grilled with Sicilian spices; the wine's dark fruit matches the meaty fish.
Accessible everyday pairing for the local cheese-topped pizza.

history.summary: Cantine Milazzo produces a Sicilia DOC Nero d'Avola as the accessible everyday wine alongside the rarer Mamertino DOC expressions. The Milazzo area's proximity to the Tyrrhenian Sea creates a cooler north-east Sicilian terroir for Nero d'Avola compared to the hotter south and centre of the island.

milestones.event: First Milazzo Nero d'Avola vintage

wines.description: Cantine Milazzo's accessible Sicilia DOC Nero d'Avola from the Tyrrhenian-influenced north-east: dark-fruited and everyday in character.

wines.name: Florio Marsala Vergine Riserva
wines.oak_regime: Minimum 10 years in large Slavonian oak barrels (botti); solera-style perpetuo system

taste.summary: Florio's Marsala Vergine Riserva is a benchmark for the driest and most complex Marsala style: fortified without added must, aged minimum 10 years in large oak in the tuff-stone cellars, developing deep rancio oxidative complexity.

pairings.why: The wine's intense oxidative complexity and dried-fruit notes pair classically with aged cheese.
The rancio richness and dried-fruit depth create a compelling match with bitter dark chocolate.
Marsala Vergine's complexity and length make it a perfect contemplative after-dinner pour.

history.summary: Cantine Florio was founded in 1833 by Vincenzo Florio, a Calabrian entrepreneur who built the most iconic Marsala house in Sicily. The historic tuff-stone cellars with 104 arches, built in 1840, house over 1,400 barrels in solera. The Vergine Riserva represents the apex of the Marsala tradition, produced without the mosto cotto or mistella additions that define lower styles.

milestones.event: Cantine Florio founded by Vincenzo Florio in Marsala
The iconic tuff-stone barrel cellars with 104 arches completed
Florio becomes one of Sicily's most powerful commercial dynasties

wines.description: Florio's benchmark Marsala Vergine Riserva: dry, oxidative Grillo aged minimum 10 years in the legendary tuff-stone Marsala cellars with 104 arches.

wines.name: Florio Targa Riserva 1840
wines.oak_regime: Minimum 5 years in large Slavonian oak barrels

taste.summary: The Targa Riserva 1840 is Florio's ambassador wine: a Marsala Superiore Riserva of medium-sweet richness, aged 5-plus years in the historic cellars, balancing caramelised dried-fruit complexity with the freshness of Grillo.

pairings.why: The wine's caramel-raisin sweetness and almond notes are the definitive match for Sicily's most famous dessert.
Florio Marsala is traditionally used in cassata itself; drinking it alongside the cake completes the pairing.
The wine's dried-fruit and nut notes are a natural companion for Sicilian dried fruits and nuts.

history.summary: The Targa Riserva 1840 was created to commemorate the year the Florio tuff-stone cellars were completed - the reference '1840' appears on the label. It is Florio's most widely distributed and internationally recognised Marsala wine, winning the Targa (award) at successive international wine competitions and becoming the modern standard for Marsala Superiore Riserva.

milestones.event: First Targa Riserva 1840 vintage; created as Florio's international-market ambassador wine

wines.description: Florio's most celebrated Marsala: Targa Riserva 1840, a Marsala Superiore Riserva of medium-sweet richness aged in the 1840-built historic tuff-stone cellars.

wines.name: Florio Terre Arse Marsala Vergine
wines.oak_regime: Minimum 7 years in large Slavonian oak barrels

taste.summary: Terre Arse (burnt lands - a reference to Marsala's sun-scorched landscape) is Florio's dry-style Marsala Vergine mid-tier: oxidative, nutty and almond-driven, without the full intensity of the Riserva.

pairings.why: The wine's almond-hazelnut notes pair seamlessly with Sicilian nut-based pastries.
Dry oxidative character and medium body balance the mild tanginess of sheep's-milk cheese.
The wine's oxidative complexity and dry fortification are a classic match with rich foie gras.

history.summary: Terre Arse was introduced to fill a mid-tier position in the Florio dry Marsala range, sitting between the standard Vergine and the Riserva. The name honours the Marsala landscape's distinctive sun-bleached, heat-scorched quality that defines the terroir of the Grillo grape.

milestones.event: Terre Arse introduced as the mid-tier Florio dry Marsala expression

wines.description: Florio's mid-tier dry Marsala Vergine: oxidative, almond-hazelnut in character, from Grillo aged in the historic Marsala tuff-stone cellars.

wines.name: Florio Morsi di Luce Passito di Pantelleria
wines.oak_regime: No oak; stainless steel maturation

taste.summary: Morsi di Luce (bites of light) is Florio's Passito di Pantelleria: sun-dried Zibibbo grapes from Pantelleria producing a sweet, honeyed wine with apricot and orange blossom aromatics.

pairings.why: Honeyed apricot sweetness complements the chocolate and ricotta filling of the cannoli.
The wine's honeyed citrus notes pair naturally with the sweet almond ices of western Sicily.
Orange blossom and candied citrus notes are a lovely match for fresh stone-fruit tarts.

history.summary: Cantine Florio extended its portfolio to Pantelleria in the late 1990s, producing a passito from Zibibbo under the Morsi di Luce label. The wine uses sun-dried grapes from the alberello pantesco trained vines on Pantelleria's volcanic soils, providing Florio's take on Sicily's most celebrated sweet wine appellation.

milestones.event: First Morsi di Luce Passito di Pantelleria vintage

wines.description: Florio's Passito di Pantelleria: sun-dried Zibibbo with honeyed apricot and orange blossom sweetness from the volcanic island of Pantelleria.

wines.name: Florio Marsala Superiore Semisecco
wines.oak_regime: Minimum 2 years in large Slavonian oak barrels

taste.summary: Florio's accessible everyday Marsala Superiore Semisecco: the classic Marsala for cooking and casual drinking, with soft caramelised-fruit character and gentle oxidative notes.

pairings.why: Marsala's caramelised sweetness and oxidative character complement the tuna-veal combination.
Marsala is a traditional tiramisu ingredient; drinking it alongside deepens the flavour experience.
The wine's herb and caramel notes bridge the sage-wrapped veal in a classic Roman preparation.

history.summary: The standard Marsala Superiore represents the everyday accessible tier in the Florio range - the Marsala most associated in kitchens across Italy and beyond. The Semisecco style bridges the dry Vergine and sweet styles, producing the versatile off-dry character most associated with the Marsala name internationally.

milestones.event: Marsala wine production begins at Florio; the Superiore becomes the commercial staple
Marsala Superiore becomes the most exported Italian wine after Chianti

wines.description: Florio's accessible everyday Marsala Superiore Semisecco: caramelised-fruit, off-dry character for cooking and casual drinking from Sicily's most storied Marsala house.

wines.name: Pellegrino Marsala Superiore Riserva 10 Anni
wines.oak_regime: Minimum 10 years in large Slavonian oak barrels; solera-style ageing

taste.summary: Pellegrino's 10-year Marsala Superiore Riserva is a showcase for the family-owned house's century-long expertise: 10 years of barrel ageing develop extraordinary complexity of dried fruit, coffee and roasted nut character.

pairings.why: The wine's caramel-coffee complexity complements the full range of traditional Sicilian pastries.
Oxidative complexity and dried-fruit richness pair with the truffle honey and aged cheese.
Coffee and roasted nut notes create a seamless match with dark chocolate confections.

history.summary: Founded in 1880, Cantine Pellegrino is now in its sixth generation of family ownership and is one of the last major independent Marsala houses. Their 10-year Superiore Riserva draws on the historic solera-style system in the Marsala cellars, which contain some of the oldest base wines in continuous use in western Sicily.

milestones.event: Cantine Pellegrino founded in Marsala
Pellegrino establishes 10-year ageing programme for the Superiore Riserva

wines.description: Pellegrino's 10-year Marsala Superiore Riserva: a century of family expertise in a rich, complex oxidative wine of dried fruit, coffee and roasted nut character.

wines.name: Pellegrino Marsala Vergine Soleras
wines.oak_regime: Minimum 5 years in large Slavonian oak; solera-style perpetuo blending

taste.summary: Pellegrino's Vergine Soleras uses a traditional solera system (perpetuo) maintaining wine from multiple decades: a layered, dry Marsala of walnut-and-apricot complexity that reflects the long Pellegrino history.

pairings.dish: Pane cunzato (Sicilian bread with oil, tomato, anchovy, aged cheese)
pairings.why: The wine's dry oxidative character and saline mineral notes match the rich, layered Sicilian street food.
In western Sicily, a small glass of dry Marsala Vergine is a traditional before-dinner pour.
The wine's dry oxidative character bridges the sweet-sour aubergine dish perfectly.

history.summary: The Pellegrino Vergine Soleras is produced using the traditional perpetuo (solera) method in which wine from multiple vintages is blended, preserving historic base wines from the founding decades of the winery. This technique is the most authentic Marsala production method, linking current production directly to the 1880 founding.

milestones.event: Pellegrino Marsala solera programme established at founding
Vergine Soleras established as the driest, most traditional Pellegrino expression

wines.description: Pellegrino's dry Marsala Vergine made via traditional solera: layered, walnut-apricot complexity drawing on wine from multiple decades in the historic Marsala cellars.

wines.name: Pellegrino Nes Passito di Pantelleria
wines.oak_regime: No oak; stainless steel; concentrated sun-drying

taste.summary: Nes (shortened from the Pantelleria dialect 'giardino pantesco', an enclosed garden) is Pellegrino's sun-drenched Passito di Pantelleria: concentrated Zibibbo with apricot-honey character and the characteristic volcanic depth of Pantelleria.

pairings.dish: Fresh ricotta tart with orange blossom
pairings.why: The wine's orange blossom aromatics are a natural match for ricotta desserts scented with orange flower water.
Almond and honey notes in the wine mirror the almond-based marzipan.
Tropical fruit notes in the wine echo the fresh pineapple; the mint provides refreshing contrast.

history.summary: Pellegrino extended its Marsala expertise to Pantelleria in the 1990s, producing the Nes Passito di Pantelleria from Zibibbo (Moscato d'Alessandria) grapes trained in the traditional alberello pantesco low-bush system on the volcanic island. The wine draws on Pellegrino's fortified-wine expertise to produce a rich, balanced Pantelleria Passito.

milestones.event: First Nes Passito di Pantelleria vintage from Pellegrino

wines.description: Pellegrino's Passito di Pantelleria from sun-dried Zibibbo: concentrated apricot-honey sweetness with the volcanic depth of the alberello pantesco-trained vines.

wines.name: Pellegrino Moscato di Pantelleria
wines.oak_regime: No oak; stainless steel

taste.summary: The Moscato di Pantelleria is a lighter, fresher sweet wine style compared to the passito: Zibibbo grapes not fully dried produce a wine of floral freshness, bright apricot character and lower alcohol, ideal as an aperitivo or light dessert wine.

pairings.why: Fresh floral and fruit sweetness mirrors the light freshness of Sicilian ices.
Floral-apricot sweetness pairs naturally with fresh stone and tropical fruits.
The wine's gentle sweetness complements light cream-filled Sicilian pastries.

history.summary: Pellegrino's Moscato di Pantelleria represents the lighter, more immediately accessible sweet wine from the island, using Zibibbo grapes not fully sun-dried. The appellation requires a minimum of 11% alcohol and allows a fresher, more floral expression than the concentrated Passito di Pantelleria style.

milestones.event: First Pellegrino Moscato di Pantelleria vintage

wines.description: Pellegrino's lighter, fresher Pantelleria sweet wine: Moscato di Pantelleria DOC from Zibibbo with floral-apricot freshness and approachable low alcohol.

wines.name: Marco De Bartoli Vecchio Samperi
wines.oak_regime: Minimum 10-30 years in a 5-tier solera system of old Slavonian oak barrels at Samperi

taste.summary: Vecchio Samperi is Marco De Bartoli's life work and Sicily's most important wine: un-fortified perpetuo Grillo aged in a 5-tier solera at Samperi, achieving Marsala-like depth and complexity entirely through natural oxidation - no fortification spirits added. Among the world's most distinctive and debated fortified-style wines.

pairings.dish: Served solo, gently chilled, as meditation wine
pairings.why: The extraordinary complexity of Vecchio Samperi rewards pure contemplative solo drinking.
The wine's extreme oxidative depth requires the most complex and intense cheese counterpart.

pairings.dish: Sicilian pantesca salad (Pantelleria) with tuna and capers
pairings.why: The saline-mineral dimension and bitter-orange notes of Vecchio Samperi pair uniquely with the island salad.

history.summary: Marco De Bartoli founded his estate at Samperi in 1978 with a singular mission: to revive the authentic, un-fortified perpetuo style of Marsala that the fortification regulations of 1969 had effectively suppressed. Unable to label the wine as Marsala DOC (because it contains no grape spirits), De Bartoli classified Vecchio Samperi as Vino da Tavola - yet it became more celebrated and more expensive than any Marsala DOC wine. The 20-year and 30-year versions represent different solera withdrawals.

milestones.event: Marco De Bartoli founded at Samperi; Vecchio Samperi concept established
First commercial release of Vecchio Samperi as Vino da Tavola
Marco De Bartoli dies; son Sebastiano continues the Samperi solera programme

wines.description: De Bartoli's masterpiece: un-fortified Grillo in a 5-tier solera at Samperi, achieving Marsala depth without fortification. One of Sicily's most singular and debated wines.

wines.name: Marco De Bartoli Marsala Superiore Riserva 10 Anni
wines.oak_regime: Minimum 10 years in small-medium old Slavonian oak barrels at Samperi

taste.summary: De Bartoli's 10-year Marsala Superiore Riserva is produced with minimal fortification compared to standard Marsala, preserving more natural character from the Grillo grape: the result is lighter in oxidative weight than the Vergine range but more genuinely grape-expressive than industrial Marsala.

pairings.dish: Ricotta con miele di zagara (ricotta with orange blossom honey)
pairings.why: The wine's dried-citrus and caramel notes pair beautifully with the Sicilian ricotta and honey classic.
Hazelnut and dried-fruit notes complement the pistachio's grassy-sweet character.
De Bartoli Marsala in both the dish and the glass provides the most authentic version of this classic preparation.

history.summary: Marco De Bartoli's DOC Marsala was produced alongside the non-DOC Vecchio Samperi to demonstrate that authentic, minimally fortified Marsala could compete with oxidative wines of global stature. The 10-year Superiore Riserva is the most accessible DOC wine from the estate and has helped rehabilitate Marsala's reputation for quality.

milestones.event: First De Bartoli Marsala Superiore Riserva vintage

wines.description: De Bartoli's artisan 10-year Marsala Superiore Riserva: minimally fortified Grillo of genuine grape-expressive complexity alongside the oxidative depth of a decade in barrel.

wines.name: Marco De Bartoli Vigna La Miccia Marsala Oro
wines.oak_regime: Minimum 3 years in old Slavonian oak

taste.summary: Vigna La Miccia is De Bartoli's single-vineyard Marsala Superiore Oro: more approachable and lighter in oxidative weight than the Riserva, produced from a specific Grillo plot near Samperi with fresher fruit character.

pairings.why: The wine's soft sweetness and gentle oxidative character complement fried Sicilian pastries.

pairings.dish: Grilled Sicilian prawns with orange butter
pairings.why: The wine's orange-citrus notes and gentle warmth pair with the orange-butter prawn dish.
The off-dry warmth and caramelised notes of the wine are classic with seared foie gras.

history.summary: Vigna La Miccia is named for the specific Grillo vineyard at Samperi from which it is exclusively sourced, making it the estate's single-vineyard expression of Marsala Superiore. Introduced in 1987, it sits between the lighter Vecchio Samperi base wines and the 10-year Riserva in style and ageing.

milestones.event: First Vigna La Miccia vintage

wines.description: De Bartoli's single-vineyard Marsala Superiore Oro from the La Miccia Grillo plot at Samperi: approachable, fresh-fruited and gently oxidative.

wines.name: Marco De Bartoli Grappoli del Grillo
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel on fine lees

taste.summary: Grappoli del Grillo is De Bartoli's dry still table wine from Grillo, made without oxidation: a fresh, mineral white that showcases the grape's natural salinity, citrus character and almond-bitter finish when vinified as a conventional still wine rather than a perpetuo.

pairings.dish: Busiate con pesto trapanese (almonds, tomato, basil)
pairings.why: The wine's almond-bitter character and Grillo's natural affinity mirror the Trapani-style almond pesto.
Saline mineral character and bright citrus acidity pair with the clean flavours of grilled cuttlefish.

pairings.dish: Insalata di arance siciliane (Sicilian orange salad)
pairings.why: The wine's orange-blossom notes and mineral freshness harmonise with sweet Sicilian orange salad.

history.summary: Marco De Bartoli introduced Grappoli del Grillo in 1990 to show the Grillo grape in its freshest, unoxidised form - a deliberate contrast to the oxidative Samperi wines. The wine demonstrates that Grillo, long dismissed as purely a Marsala base grape, is also a serious dry white wine variety of mineral character.

milestones.event: First Grappoli del Grillo vintage; Grillo repositioned as a dry table wine variety

wines.description: De Bartoli's dry still Grillo table wine: a deliberate contrast to Vecchio Samperi, showcasing the grape's mineral freshness, citrus character and almond-bitter finish.

wines.name: Marco De Bartoli Marsala Superiore Riserva Oro
wines.oak_regime: Minimum 5 years in old Slavonian oak barrels at Samperi

taste.summary: De Bartoli's Marsala Superiore Riserva Oro is an approachable 5-year expression: lighter oxidative weight than the 10-year Riserva but with the estate's characteristic Grillo purity and a golden-almond character that bridges the aperitivo and dessert register.

pairings.dish: Torrone di mandorle siciliano (Sicilian almond nougat)
pairings.why: Golden-almond notes in the wine pair perfectly with Sicily's beloved almond nougat.
The wine's warming sweetness and dried-fruit notes are a natural companion for nut-based pastries.
The wine's golden sweetness and gentle acidity elevate the combination of fresh fruit and creamy ricotta.

history.summary: The Marsala Superiore Riserva Oro is De Bartoli's 5-year version of the Marsala DOC range, providing an accessible introduction to the Samperi style at a shorter ageing period than the 10-year expression. Introduced in the early 1980s to fill a mid-tier position, it uses the same Grillo from the Samperi estate but with a shorter barrel tenure.

milestones.event: First De Bartoli Marsala Superiore Riserva Oro vintage

wines.description: De Bartoli's 5-year Marsala Superiore Riserva Oro: accessible golden-almond Grillo from Samperi, warmer and lighter-oxidative than the 10-year expression.

wines.name: Donnafugata Ben Rye Passito di Pantelleria
wines.oak_regime: No oak; stainless steel; sun-dried Zibibbo concentration

taste.summary: Ben Rye (Arabic for 'son of the wind') is Italy's most celebrated Passito di Pantelleria: sun-dried Zibibbo from the alberello pantesco vines on the volcanic island, achieving a concentration and aromatic complexity that has defined the appellation's international reputation.

pairings.dish: Cannoli di ricotta with candied orange
pairings.why: The wine's orange blossom and apricot sweetness perfectly bridge the ricotta-filled cannolo and its candied peel.
Honeyed almond notes in the wine mirror the almond semifreddo in a harmonious resonance.
The wine's concentrated sweetness and exotic spices create a powerful contrast with the pungent blue cheese.

history.summary: Ben Rye was first produced in 1990 when the Rallo family's Donnafugata estate began farming Zibibbo on Pantelleria, trained in the traditional alberello pantesco system. The name comes from Arabic, reflecting Pantelleria's North African cultural heritage, and the wine has consistently won Gambero Rosso's highest award (Tre Bicchieri) making it the most frequently decorated sweet wine in Italy.

milestones.event: First Ben Rye vintage; Donnafugata Pantelleria production begins
Ben Rye wins first Tre Bicchieri from Gambero Rosso; begins building international reputation

wines.description: Italy's most celebrated Passito di Pantelleria: sun-dried Zibibbo with extraordinary apricot-honey concentration, first produced in 1990 and consistently regarded as the benchmark for the appellation.

wines.name: Donnafugata Kabir Moscato di Pantelleria
wines.oak_regime: No oak; stainless steel

taste.summary: Kabir is Donnafugata's lighter Pantelleria sweet wine: fresher and less concentrated than Ben Rye, from Zibibbo grapes dried for a shorter period, producing a floral, juicy dessert wine of great charm.

pairings.why: The wine's floral freshness and light sweetness are a natural companion for fresh fruit.
The wine's brightness and medium sweetness pair perfectly with fruity frozen desserts.
Floral apricot notes and gentle honey sweetness complement Sicily's almond pastry tradition.

history.summary: Kabir (from the Arabic for 'great') was introduced in 1998 as the more approachable companion to Ben Rye in the Donnafugata Pantelleria range, using Zibibbo grapes with a shorter drying period to preserve more fresh fruit character. The wine fills the gap between the full intensity of a Passito di Pantelleria and a regular dry white.

milestones.event: First Kabir Moscato di Pantelleria vintage

wines.description: Donnafugata's lighter Pantelleria sweet wine: fresh, floral Moscato di Pantelleria DOC with apricot-jasmine character and approachable sweetness.

wines.name: Donnafugata Lighea
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel on lees; no drying

taste.summary: Lighea is Donnafugata's dry-to-off-dry Zibibbo table wine from Pantelleria: fresh, aromatic, with the grape's characteristic muscat-like floral and apricot character in a lighter, immediately approachable style.

pairings.dish: Grilled prawns with garlic and lemon
pairings.why: Floral aromatics and light sweetness complement the sweet-savoury grilled prawns.
The wine's fragrant floral notes and light body pair with the aromatic vegetable couscous.

pairings.dish: Fresh soft cheese with herb honey
pairings.why: Off-dry sweetness and floral aromatics bridge the creamy cheese and fragrant honey.

history.summary: Lighea (named for the mermaid of a Tomasi di Lampedusa novella set on Sicily) is Donnafugata's most approachable Zibibbo expression, made without drying as a fresh table wine. It was introduced to offer a lighter entry into the Donnafugata Pantelleria range for everyday drinking alongside the more intense sweet wines.

milestones.event: First Lighea Zibibbo table wine vintage

wines.description: Donnafugata's fresh Zibibbo table wine from Pantelleria: aromatic, off-dry and immediate, showcasing the grape's floral-apricot character without drying.

wines.name: Marco De Bartoli Bukkuram Sole d'Agosto
wines.oak_regime: No oak; minimal intervention; no sulphur

taste.summary: Sole d'Agosto (August sun) is the De Bartoli minimal-intervention take on Passito di Pantelleria: zero-sulphur Zibibbo from the Bukkuram estate, producing an almost overwhelmingly concentrated dried-fruit and honey wine with a volcanic mineral dimension absent from other Pantelleria passiti.

pairings.why: The extraordinary concentration and volcanic mineral depth reward contemplative solo drinking.
The wine's intense sweetness and mineral depth create a powerful contrast with pungent Gorgonzola.
The wine's honeyed sweetness and almond notes bridge perfectly with the frozen almond dessert.

history.summary: Sebastiano De Bartoli produces Sole d'Agosto from the family's Bukkuram estate on Pantelleria using the same minimal-intervention philosophy that defines the Samperi Marsala operation: no added sulphur, late harvesting and traditional alberello pantesco cultivation. The result is among the most intense and uncompromising expressions of the Passito di Pantelleria appellation.

milestones.event: First Sole d'Agosto vintage from the Bukkuram Pantelleria estate

wines.description: De Bartoli's zero-sulphur, minimal-intervention Passito di Pantelleria: extreme dried-fruit and honey concentration with a volcanic mineral dimension from the Bukkuram estate.

wines.name: Marco De Bartoli Pietra Pumice
wines.oak_regime: No oak; stainless steel; no sulphur; no fining

taste.summary: Pietra Pumice (pumice stone) is De Bartoli's dry Zibibbo table wine from Pantelleria: made without additions on the volcanic pumice soils, it shows the wild mineral character of the island when Zibibbo is vinified dry and naturally.

pairings.why: Volcanic mineral and herbal notes from the pumice soils pair with the aromatic spiced fish couscous.
Mineral-saline character and high acidity frame the meaty swordfish and sweet-sour caponata.

pairings.dish: Pantelleria pantesca salad (tuna, capers, olives, tomato)
pairings.why: The wine's volcanic mineral and herbal character mirrors the island's signature salad.

history.summary: Pietra Pumice was introduced to show the dry potential of Zibibbo on Pantelleria's distinctive pumice volcanic soils. Farmed without additions under the De Bartoli minimal-intervention philosophy, the wine reveals the wild mineral character of the island entirely different from its sweet incarnation in Ben Rye or Sole d'Agosto.

milestones.event: First Pietra Pumice vintage; dry Zibibbo from Pantelleria established as a serious proposition

wines.description: De Bartoli's dry zero-addition Zibibbo from Pantelleria's pumice soils: volcanic mineral and wild herb character, Zibibbo in its most mineral and natural form.

wines.name: Serragghia Passito di Pantelleria
wines.oak_regime: No oak; no additions; no sulphur; traditional alberello pantesco sun-drying

taste.summary: Gabrio Bini's Serragghia is the cult natural passito of Pantelleria: zero-sulphur, zero additions, biodynamic Zibibbo on volcanic pumice soils producing a wine of wild, unpredictable character and extraordinary mineral depth - the most challenging and most sought-after natural sweet wine in Sicily.

pairings.why: Serragghia's wild variability and mineral depth reward solo contemplation without distraction.

pairings.dish: Pantelleria olive bruschetta with caper salt
pairings.why: The wine's volcanic mineral and wild herb notes bridge the island's most authentic bread snack.
The wine's extreme concentration and mineral depth need the most complex aged cheese as a counterpart.

history.summary: Gabrio Bini produces Serragghia from old Zibibbo vines in the Contrada Khamma on Pantelleria using no sulphur, no additions and biodynamic farming in the alberello pantesco system. The wine is made in tiny quantities and distributed almost exclusively through natural wine specialist importers. It has cult status in the natural wine world far exceeding its production volume.

milestones.event: First Serragghia Passito di Pantelleria vintage by Gabrio Bini

wines.description: Gabrio Bini's cult zero-sulphur biodynamic Passito di Pantelleria: wild, unpredictable and extraordinarily mineral, the most challenging and sought-after natural sweet wine in Sicily.

wines.name: Serragghia Zibibbo Secco
wines.oak_regime: No oak; stainless steel; no additions; no sulphur

taste.summary: Serragghia's dry Zibibbo is among the most mineral and wild table wines produced in Sicily: zero-sulphur, biodynamic, from the volcanic pumice soils of Pantelleria, it captures the island's raw elemental character without sweetness.

pairings.why: The wine's volcanic mineral and maquis notes mirror the island's signature salad of tuna, tomato, capers and olives.
Saline mineral character and wild herb notes frame the oceanic sweetness of the raw shrimp.
The wine's volcanic depth and saline character are a natural match for char-grilled octopus.

history.summary: Gabrio Bini introduced a dry Zibibbo from the Serragghia estate in 2008, providing a non-sweet expression of the island's character. The wine is made with the same biodynamic, zero-sulphur approach as the Passito but captures the wild mineral soul of Pantelleria without sweetness, becoming sought-after in natural wine circles.

milestones.event: First Serragghia dry Zibibbo vintage

wines.description: Gabrio Bini's zero-sulphur biodynamic dry Zibibbo from Pantelleria's pumice soils: wild, mineral and saline - the island's elemental character without sweetness.

wines.name: Hauner Malvasia delle Lipari Passito
wines.oak_regime: No oak; stainless steel; sun-dried on mats for 15-20 days

taste.summary: Hauner's Malvasia delle Lipari Passito is the benchmark for this rare Aeolian appellation: sun-dried Malvasia di Lipari producing a wine of extraordinary floral complexity, apricot sweetness and the distinctive volcanic mineral character of the island of Salina.

pairings.dish: Sicilian cassata with ricotta and marzipan
pairings.why: The wine's floral-honeyed sweetness and volcanic mineral depth are the perfect companion to Sicily's most elaborate dessert.
The wine's sweetness and mineral notes bridge the sharp aged cheese and the briny island capers.
Floral apricot sweetness and volcanic mineral notes mirror the citrus-bitter complexity of bergamot granita.

history.summary: Carlo Hauner arrived on Salina from Germany in the 1960s as a painter and graphic artist, fell in love with the island and its wines, and established Hauner in 1963. He revived the traditional sun-drying of Malvasia di Lipari grapes that had been in decline, producing the wine that would define Malvasia delle Lipari DOC as a serious dessert wine appellation. The estate is now run by his son Carlo Jr.

milestones.event: Hauner estate founded on Salina; Malvasia delle Lipari revived
Malvasia delle Lipari DOC officially established, partly as a result of Hauner's advocacy
Hauner Passito wins Gambero Rosso Tre Bicchieri; Aeolian wines gain international recognition

wines.description: The benchmark Malvasia delle Lipari Passito: Hauner's sun-dried Malvasia di Lipari from Salina in the Aeolian Islands, floral-honeyed with extraordinary volcanic mineral complexity.

verified.address_quoted: Via Umberto I 83, 98050 Santa Marina di Salina (ME)

wines.name: Hauner Malvasia delle Lipari Naturale
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel; partial drying

taste.summary: The Naturale style is a lighter, fresher take on Malvasia delle Lipari: less concentrated than the Passito but showing the grape's characteristic floral-apricot aromatics and the volcanic salinity of the Aeolian Islands in a more immediately approachable register.

pairings.dish: Aeolian bruschetta with oregano and tomatoes
pairings.why: The wine's light sweetness and saline mineral notes bridge the herb-tomato bruschetta.
Floral sweetness and apricot character are a delicate match for Aeolian almond sweets.

pairings.dish: Fresh fruit with sheep's milk yogurt
pairings.why: The wine's floral-sweet character and light mineral acidity complement the tangy yogurt and fresh fruit.

history.summary: Hauner's Naturale is the lighter, fresher expression of the Malvasia delle Lipari range, using grapes with a shorter or lighter drying period than the Passito. It provides a more everyday entry into the Aeolian sweet wine tradition, capturing the floral character of Malvasia di Lipari without the full concentration of the Passito.

milestones.event: Malvasia delle Lipari DOC established; Naturale style recognised alongside Passito and Liquoroso

wines.description: Hauner's lighter Malvasia delle Lipari Naturale: fresher and more approachable than the Passito, with floral-apricot sweetness and saline mineral from the Aeolian Islands.

verified.address_quoted: Via Umberto I 83, 98050 Santa Marina di Salina (ME)

wines.name: Hauner Salina Rosso
wines.oak_regime: 10 months in large oak botti

taste.summary: Hauner's Salina Rosso is a rare Aeolian red from Nero d'Avola and the indigenous Corinto Nero: light-bodied, herb-scented and saline-mineral, reflecting the island's volcanic coastal terroir in a red wine format.

pairings.dish: Aeolian grilled fish with capers and olives
pairings.why: Light body and saline mineral notes are the natural companion for island-style grilled fish.

pairings.dish: Pasta with Aeolian pesto (capers, olives, tomato)
pairings.why: The wine's herb and light tannin frame the vibrant Aeolian pesto.
The wine's saline character echoes the intense briny anchovy and caper combination.

history.summary: Hauner produced a small amount of Salina Rosso to offer island visitors a local red wine alongside the celebrated Malvasia. The blend of Nero d'Avola and the rare indigenous Corinto Nero (related to Corinthian currant grapes) reflects the Aeolian tradition of co-fermenting whatever grew on the island.

milestones.event: First Hauner Salina Rosso vintage

wines.description: Hauner's rare Aeolian red from Nero d'Avola and indigenous Corinto Nero on Salina: light-bodied, herb-scented and saline-mineral from the island volcanic terroir.

verified.address_quoted: Via Umberto I 83, 98050 Santa Marina di Salina (ME)

wines.name: Caravaglio Malvasia delle Lipari Passito
wines.oak_regime: No oak; stainless steel; traditional alberello sun-drying

taste.summary: Caravaglio's organic Malvasia delle Lipari Passito captures the pure floral-honeyed character of the grape from the Malfa area on Salina's northern slope, with a distinctive volcanic mineral dimension and slightly more freshness than the Hauner style.

pairings.why: The wine's honeyed-floral sweetness bridges the creamy ricotta and crispy pastry.
Orange blossom and honey notes are a natural match for almond-based desserts.
The classic sweet-and-savory contrast of sweet passito with pungent blue cheese.

history.summary: The second-generation Caravaglio family on Salina produces organic Malvasia delle Lipari from vines farmed sustainably on the northern slope of the island at Malfa. Their passito offers a slightly fresher, more immediately approachable style than Hauner's while maintaining the distinctive floral-honeyed character of the appellation.

milestones.event: First Caravaglio Malvasia delle Lipari Passito vintage

wines.description: Caravaglio's organic Malvasia delle Lipari Passito from Salina's northern slope: floral-honeyed sweetness with volcanic mineral depth and organic freshness.

wines.name: Caravaglio Salina Bianco
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel on fine lees

taste.summary: Caravaglio's Salina Bianco is a dry expression of Malvasia di Lipari: the grape's characteristic floral and apricot aromatics expressed without sweetness, blended with Inzolia for freshness. A charming, mineral everyday white from the Aeolian Islands.

pairings.dish: Spaghetti con bottarga di muggine (mullet roe pasta)
pairings.why: Floral aromatics and saline mineral character frame the intense salted mullet roe pasta.
The wine's island mineral character and floral notes are the natural match for Aeolian fish preparations.
Floral freshness and bright acidity bridge the sweet-sour island caponata.

history.summary: Caravaglio introduced a dry Salina Bianco in 2000 to offer a table wine alongside the sweet Malvasia, drawing on Malvasia di Lipari for aromatics and Inzolia for structure and freshness. The wine captures the Aeolian Islands' character in a dry everyday format.

milestones.event: First Caravaglio Salina Bianco vintage

wines.description: Caravaglio's dry Aeolian everyday white: Malvasia di Lipari and Inzolia from Salina, floral and mineral - the island's character without sweetness.

wines.name: Tasca d'Almerita Capofaro Malvasia delle Lipari
wines.oak_regime: No oak; stainless steel; alberello pantesco sun-drying

taste.summary: Capofaro's Malvasia delle Lipari Passito is produced at one of Italy's most dramatically situated wineries, with terrace vines overlooking Stromboli. The Tasca d'Almerita winemaking precision elevates the passito style with clean, precise floral-honeyed concentration and the distinctive volcanic mineral signature of Salina.

pairings.dish: Cannoli di ricotta con scorzette di arancia
pairings.why: The wine's orange blossom and candied citrus notes are the definitive match for Sicilian ricotta cannoli.
Floral honey notes and apricot sweetness complement the pistachio's grassy richness.
The wine's floral sweetness and volcanic mineral note bridge the fresh pastry and berry combination.

history.summary: Tasca d'Almerita established the Capofaro estate on Salina in 2000, creating what is now recognised as one of Italy's most dramatic wine-hotel experiences. The Malvasia delle Lipari Passito was produced from the first vintage in 2002, drawing on the estate's terrace vineyards trained in the traditional alberello pantesco system with the Tyrrhenian Sea as backdrop.

milestones.event: Tasca d'Almerita acquires Capofaro on Salina
First Capofaro Malvasia delle Lipari Passito vintage

wines.description: Tasca d'Almerita's Malvasia delle Lipari Passito from the Capofaro estate on Salina: precise floral-honeyed concentration with volcanic mineral depth from Stromboli-view terrace vines.

wines.name: Cantina Rallo Marsala Superiore
wines.oak_regime: Minimum 2 years in large Slavonian oak barrels

taste.summary: Rallo's Marsala Superiore is the historic house's standard fortified expression: accessible, caramelised-fruit and gently oxidative from the 1860-founded Marsala cellar.

pairings.why: Marsala is the defining ingredient in zabaglione; drinking Rallo alongside deepens the experience.
The classic Italian dessert uses Marsala; Rallo's accessible style matches perfectly.
Rallo Marsala in both the cooking and the glass is the most authentic pairing for this classic dish.

history.summary: Cantina Rallo was founded in 1860 by Diego Rallo, making it one of the oldest Marsala houses still in operation. Acquired by the Vesco family in 1996, the estate continues to produce accessible Marsala alongside Sicilian table wines from Alcamo and Pantelleria.

milestones.event: Cantina Rallo founded by Diego Rallo in Marsala
Vesco family acquires Rallo; estate range expanded

wines.description: Cantina Rallo's accessible Marsala Superiore from the 1860-founded house: caramelised-fruit and gently oxidative, the everyday Marsala from western Sicily.

wines.name: Cantina Rallo Alcamo Bianco
wines.oak_regime: Stainless steel

taste.summary: Rallo's Alcamo Bianco is a fresh, everyday Sicilian white from the historic Alcamo DOC near Trapani: Catarratto and Grillo delivering a clean, citrus-fresh wine at an accessible price.

pairings.why: Fresh citrus acidity and clean mineral notes bridge the briny clam sweetness.

pairings.dish: Grilled sea bass with olive oil
pairings.why: Light body and clean mineral character are natural companions for simple grilled fish.
The wine's refreshing freshness cuts through the oils in mixed seafood salad.

history.summary: Rallo's Alcamo Bianco is produced from Catarratto and Grillo vineyards in the Alcamo DOC zone of western Sicily, drawing on the historic Trapani province wine tradition. The wine provides an affordable, everyday introduction to western Sicilian white wine at the estate's most accessible price point.

milestones.event: First Rallo Alcamo Bianco vintage in the modern estate bottling era

wines.description: Rallo's everyday Alcamo DOC white: Catarratto and Grillo from western Sicily near Trapani, clean and citrus-fresh at an accessible price.

wines.name: COS Cerasuolo di Vittoria Classico DOCG
wines.oak_regime: Aged in large Slavonian oak casks, 12-14 months

taste.summary: Sicily's most celebrated DOCG in its defining expression: Nero d'Avola structure lifted by Frappato's sour-cherry acidity, earthy-mineral finish from Vittoria clay-limestone soils.

pairings.why: The wine's bright acidity and red-fruit cut through the rich rice and meat filling.
Frappato's herbal freshness complements fennel; Nero d'Avola's grip holds the fat.
Light game meat matches the wine's earthy red-fruit register perfectly.

history.summary: COS was founded in 1980 by three school friends at Vittoria and began producing Cerasuolo di Vittoria DOCG from the start. The winery shifted to biodynamic farming in the early 2000s and pioneered amphora vinification on the island, but the Cerasuolo Classico has always been their flagship DOCG expression.

milestones.event: COS founded; first Cerasuolo di Vittoria produced
Biodynamic conversion begins across the estate
Cerasuolo di Vittoria granted DOCG status, Sicily's first and only

wines.description: Sicily's only DOCG in its benchmark expression: 60% Nero d'Avola and 40% Frappato from biodynamically farmed Vittoria vineyards, aged in large Slavonian oak.

wines.name: COS Frappato
wines.oak_regime: Aged in concrete vats and large Slavonian oak, approximately 8 months

taste.summary: Pure Frappato of haunting lightness: pale ruby, white-pepper-dusted red berry fruit, racy acidity and a mineral finish that evokes iron-rich Vittoria soils.

pairings.why: The wine's lightness and acidity bridge the oily fish with the sweet-sour vegetables.
Frappato's delicate red fruit complements grilled swordfish without overpowering.
Low tannin and vibrant acidity pair seamlessly with creamy fresh cheese.

history.summary: COS began bottling Frappato as a varietal in the late 1980s, recognizing the grape's unique light-bodied quality before it gained wider attention. The biodynamic conversion amplified the mineral character that has made this one of Italy's most distinctive light reds.

milestones.event: First varietal Frappato bottled at COS
Biodynamic farming applied to Frappato vineyards

wines.description: 100% Frappato from COS's biodynamic Vittoria estate: pale, peppery, fresh-fruited light red of great purity and mineral acidity.

wines.name: COS Rami
wines.oak_regime: Fermented and aged in stainless steel; partial concrete-egg aging

taste.summary: COS's white blend shows Sicilian native whites at their most expressive: peachy Inzolia rounds Grecanico's herbaceous mineral edge, finishing with sea-salt salinity.

pairings.dish: Grilled branzino with capers and lemon
pairings.why: The wine's salinity and citrus mirror the capers while its body supports the fish.
Fennel-forward aroma echoes the sardine-fennel combination of the classic Palermo pasta.
Bright acidity cleanses the palate between varied seafood textures.

history.summary: Rami was developed by COS to showcase the native Sicilian white grapes Inzolia and Grecanico grown organically on the Vittoria estate. The blend reflects COS's philosophy of minimal intervention and native-variety focus.

milestones.event: First vintage of Rami from organic Inzolia and Grecanico

wines.description: COS's native-white blend of Inzolia and Grecanico: mineral, saline and citrus-forward, grown biodynamically at Vittoria.

wines.name: COS Pithos Rosso
wines.oak_regime: Fermented and aged in terracotta amphorae (pithos), no oak

taste.summary: Nero d'Avola fermented and aged in terracotta amphorae without oak: the clay vessel imparts earthy minerality and textural grip unique among Sicilian reds.

pairings.why: Firm tannins cut through lamb fat; earthy minerality mirrors the herb crust.
The wine's tannic structure and salinity complement the sheep's-milk intensity.

pairings.dish: Grilled eggplant with tomato and basil
pairings.why: Earthy red fruit bridges the smoky aubergine and fresh tomato.

history.summary: COS introduced amphora fermentation around 2000, drawing on ancient Sicilian and Mediterranean vinification traditions. The Pithos Rosso from Nero d'Avola became one of Italy's most discussed natural wines and a key reference for the amphora wine movement globally.

milestones.event: First Pithos Rosso vinified in terracotta amphorae

wines.description: Nero d'Avola fermented and aged in terracotta amphorae at COS: a benchmark Italian natural wine with earthy grip and mineral depth.

wines.name: COS Pithos Bianco
wines.oak_regime: Fermented on skins and aged in terracotta amphorae, no oak

taste.summary: Grecanico fermented on skins in amphora: a paradigmatic skin-contact white of honeyed stone-fruit richness, firm phenolic texture and saline-mineral finish.

pairings.dish: Grilled octopus with lemon and olive oil
pairings.why: The wine's phenolic grip and salinity echo the char and brine of the octopus.
Skin-contact tannins complement the richness of fried aubergine and aged ricotta.
Beeswax and dried-fruit notes are a classical pairing with aged caciocavallo.

history.summary: Alongside the Pithos Rosso, COS launched the Pithos Bianco from organically farmed Grecanico, macerated on skins in terracotta. Both wines were foundational in establishing the international orange-wine movement and Italian skin-contact categories.

milestones.event: First skin-contact Grecanico vinified in amphora at COS

wines.description: Grecanico macerated on skins in terracotta amphorae at COS: a landmark Italian orange wine of textural complexity and mineral depth.

wines.name: COS Nero di Lupo
wines.oak_regime: Aged in large Slavonian oak casks, 10-12 months

taste.summary: COS's varietal Nero d'Avola reveals the grape at its richest: dark fruit, licorice and tobacco with supple biodynamic-farmed tannins and a persistent warm finish.

pairings.dish: Braised beef with tomato and olives
pairings.why: Full-bodied dark fruit matches the richness of slow-braised beef ragu.
Licorice and tobacco notes find harmony with the sharp aged cheese character.
The wine's dark fruit and earthy depth complement aubergine and tomato sauce.

history.summary: COS produces Nero di Lupo as their entry into varietal Nero d'Avola, allowing the grape's natural warmth and dark-fruit intensity to shine without amphora influence. It demonstrates that biodynamic farming yields supple rather than heavy wines even from this full-ripening variety.

milestones.event: First vintage of Nero di Lupo varietal Nero d'Avola

wines.description: COS's varietal Nero d'Avola: biodynamically farmed in Vittoria, dark-fruited and warm with supple tannin.

wines.name: Occhipinti SP68 Rosso
wines.oak_regime: Aged in large old oak casks, 8-10 months

taste.summary: Named after the SP68 road through Vittoria, Occhipinti's signature blend is a Frappato-dominant light red of extraordinary mineral lift, wild-berry purity and racy acidity.

pairings.why: Frappato's brightness and Nero d'Avola's depth together navigate the sardine-fennel-currant complexity.
Light body and racy acidity complement grilled fish without overpowering the herb-lemon sauce.
The wine's delicate tannins and red-berry fruit pair beautifully with the creamy cheese.

history.summary: Arianna Occhipinti started making wine in 2004 at age 21, naming her debut bottling for the provincial road her vines border. SP68 Rosso blends Frappato and Nero d'Avola from organically farmed low-yielding plots and became Italy's most celebrated natural light red within a decade.

milestones.event: First SP68 Rosso produced from Occhipinti's 1-hectare plot
